stem: How is pixel dimension along one in-plane axis determined?

options.text: Matrix along that axis divided by field of view
Slice thickness multiplied by receiver bandwidth
Field of view along that axis divided by the matrix along that axis
Echo time divided by repetition time

explanation: Dividing the physical field of view by the number of reconstructed samples gives pixel size. Smaller pixels can be obtained with a smaller FOV or a larger matrix.

stem: Which set of parameters defines the nominal voxel volume in a two-dimensional acquisition?

options.text: TR, TE, and flip angle
Receiver bandwidth, dwell time, and averages
B0 strength, B1 strength, and gradient slew rate
Frequency pixel size, phase pixel size, and slice thickness

explanation: A voxel is the three-dimensional tissue volume represented by a sample: two in-plane pixel dimensions times slice thickness. Sequence timing controls contrast rather than geometric voxel volume.

stem: At fixed field of view, what is a major consequence of increasing the matrix?

options.text: Smaller pixels improve spatial resolution but reduce signal per pixel
Larger pixels improve spatial resolution and SNR simultaneously
Slice thickness automatically increases
Chemical shift is eliminated without other changes

explanation: A larger matrix divides the same field of view into smaller pixels, improving detail. Smaller sampled tissue volumes contain less signal, and increasing phase matrix also lengthens acquisition.

options.text: The range of T1 values accepted into an image
The range of frequencies sampled during signal readout
The RF frequencies transmitted during every phase step
The physical width of the selected slice

explanation: Receiver bandwidth defines the frequency range accepted and digitized during readout. It is related to sampling rate and dwell time, and it affects noise and frequency-direction artifacts.

stem: Which parameter combination is internally consistent?

options.text: High receiver bandwidth with long dwell time
Low receiver bandwidth with zero sampling interval
High receiver bandwidth with short dwell time
Receiver bandwidth independent of sampling rate

explanation: A wider frequency range requires faster sampling, so high bandwidth corresponds to a short interval between samples. Lower bandwidth permits longer dwell time.

stem: At an unchanged matrix, what happens when the in-plane field of view is reduced?

options.text: Pixel size increases and spatial resolution improves
Pixel size is unchanged and slice thickness doubles
Spatial resolution worsens while SNR always increases
Pixel size decreases and spatial resolution improves

explanation: A smaller FOV divided by the same matrix yields smaller pixels and finer nominal resolution. SNR generally falls because each pixel contains less tissue, and wrap risk may increase.
